ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 102263004 refers to eggs as a food category; used as closest parent as no chicken-egg-specific code is available.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large chicken egg weighing approximately 50 g, as commonly served as a side dish or topping in Korean bibimbap or rice bowl dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, whole, raw, fresh (FDC ID 748967)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA Foundation Foods entry for whole raw chicken egg used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent the edible portion of a whole egg per 100 g. This is a raw ingredient record; cooking method may alter final nutrient values slightly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Whole eggs contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (0.72 g per 100 g) and have a glycemic index of effectively 0. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently report that eggs do not raise blood glucose and are assigned a GI of 0 (or not measurable). Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for foods with no glycemic carbohydrates, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is set to low. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 reflecting the negligible carbohydrate content per 50 g serving.

hypertension.guidance: Eggs contain approximately 142 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily sodium intake and avoid high-sodium preparation methods such as salting or adding soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Eggs have a glycemic index of effectively zero and do not raise postprandial blood glucose, making them a suitable high-protein food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; portion size and cooking method should be considered.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Eggs contain approximately 372 mg cholesterol and 3.13 g saturated fat per 100 g; current evidence suggests moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is acceptable for most individuals with cardiovascular disease, but those with dyslipidaemia should consult a clinician.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A chicken egg (달걀 or 계란 in Korean) is a whole hen egg commonly used as a protein-rich ingredient and side dish across Korean cuisine, including as a topping or component in bibimbap (비빔밥) and rice bowl dishes. In the Korean context, eggs may be served fried (gyeran-mari, 계란말이), steamed (gyeran-jjim, 계란찜), boiled, or raw as a topping on hot rice. Nutritionally, a whole egg per 100 g provides approximately 143 kcal, 12.6 g protein, 9.5 g fat, and only 0.72 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, high-protein food. Eggs are an excellent source of vitamin B12, vitamin A, vitamin D, selenium, phosphorus, and choline. The glycemic index of eggs is effectively zero due to negligible digestible carbohydrate content, making them highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-glycaemic diets. The cholesterol content (approximately 372 mg per 100 g) has historically raised cardiovascular concerns, but current dietary guidelines generally support moderate egg consumption as part of a balanced diet. Eggs are a major allergen and must be declared on food labels. They are naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and are suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians.
common_user_queries: How many calories in an egg?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods (FDC ID 748967), which is an approved primary source for raw staple ingredients. GI evidence is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Ontology codes are verified against FoodOn and FoodEx2 published vocabularies.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no vitamin C (naturally zero). trans_fat_g is 0.04 g per 100 g (naturally occurring trace trans fat from ruminant-related lipid metabolism, not industrial trans fat). glycemic_index is set to 1 (sentinel value representing effectively zero GI) as eggs contain negligible glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 reflecting negligible carbohydrate in a 50 g serving. The food_name in the input JSON was 'Egg 鸡蛋'; canonical English name has been set to 'Chicken Egg' for precision. The cuisine tag is Korean as the record originates from a Korean bibimbap restaurant on field survey data; however, the egg itself is a universal ingredient. Multilingual coverage follows Korean cuisine rules (Korean name mandatory); Chinese names are included as they appeared in the original input record. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and are not sentinel -1 values.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Set canonical food_name to 'Chicken Egg' from input 'Egg 鸡蛋'. Added Korean local name 달걀 and 계란. Added Chinese simplified and traditional. Populated full nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central FDC 748967. Set glycemic_index to 0 (incorrectly, should have been 1) per zero-GI convention. Documented dietary_fibre_g and vitamin_c_mg as naturally zero. Added FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT ontology. Completed health_context for all three conditions. Wrote LLM description exceeding 200 characters. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases (initial check was flawed).

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback. Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1 as per the 'Zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule' for effectively zero-GI foods. Added 'Dalgyal' to the flat 'aliases' list to ensure perfect consistency with 'aliases_by_language' entries, resolving the q8 failure. Updated iteration count and timestamp. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass.
